original_abstract:
The policing of rape is currently in a state of systemic crisis, characterized by a staggering "justice gap" where low conviction rates meet deeply traumatic experiences for victim-survivors. Despite decades of legal reform and policy adjustments, the criminal justice process remains an uphill struggle for the majority of those who report sexual violence. Traditional policing models, designed around the maintenance of public order and reactive responses to "stranger danger," have historically marginalized the private and complex nature of sexual offending.

In this work, the authors challenge the conventional inquiry into why rape is difficult to investigate, shifting the focus to how institutional police structures and "default" professional behaviors hinder effective outcomes. Drawing on pioneering research from Operation Soteria Bluestone—a major academic-police collaboration—this book presents a transformative six-pillar framework designed to reset the police response. The proposed model advocates for a fundamental shift toward suspect-focused investigations that prioritize perpetrator behavior over victim credibility, proactive prevention strategies to disrupt known offenders, and an engagement model rooted in procedural justice.

By integrating feminist scholarship with organizational psychology and policing data analytics, the authors outline a sustainable path for radical institutional change. This framework emphasizes that professionalizing rape investigations requires more than just training; it necessitates a complete overhaul of organizational responsibility, officer well-being, and the technological handling of digital evidence. *Policing Rape: The Way Forward* provides an essential blueprint for researchers, policymakers, and practitioners dedicated to restoring public trust and delivering genuine justice for survivors of sexual violence.
humanized_abstract:
In many respects the policing of rape is in a state of crisis. The gap between the rates of recorded convictions for rape and the routine experience of trauma and injustice suffered by victim-survivors on a daily basis is a gross one. Despite significant legislative and policy reforms over many decades, the criminal justice process is a very fraught one for the great majority of victims of rape who try to report what has been done to them. There is a very long history of policing practices that reflect a focus on the maintenance of public order, with practices that respond to perceived threats of criminal violence centred on the myth of 'stranger danger', in a context where sexual crime is often seen as being a highly personal matter and is therefore rendered both highly stigmatised and difficult to report to the authorities.

The authors challenge the everyday policing discourses surrounding the investigation of rape and challenge the explanations given as to why it is a notoriously difficult crime to investigate. They critique the internal and external mechanisms of the police institution and the default professional practices within it, that prevent success in this field of work. Grounded in the pioneering research undertaken in collaboration with Operation Soteria Bluestone, the work here develops a six-pillar conceptualisation which can act as a toolkit to assist in the achievement of an alternative policing practice. This comprises a fundamental shift to an investigative method that takes the focus from the victims to the behaviour of the alleged perpetrators of the offence. This together with preventive work to incapacitate repeat offenders and a procedural justice focussed partnership model which affords greater dignity, choice and dignity for victims of rape is shown to act as the catalyst to achieving a meaningful justice based intervention in these highly contentious and sensitive crimes.

Solving the Puzzle of Radical Change In this important new book, feminist organisational psychology and crime analytics are integrated to propose a sustainable route for radical change. Drawing on real life policing data analytics examples, the book discusses the potential for the professionalisation of investigations into rape and other sexual offences in earnest. The solutions for increasing victim satisfaction, offender detection rates and improved policing productivity at the scene of crime are complex. Training alone will not suffice as the changes required are more far reaching and require a substantial transformation of: organisational functioning; policing and victim welfare; and the digital forensic processes and technologies which underpin modern policing investigations of sexual crimes. Policing Rape: The Way Forward will be of considerable interest to academics, policy-makers and policing professionals keen to contribute to improving the criminal justice process for victims of rape and sexual violence across the world.
